records.query: What is a backhaul and why does it matter to a carrier?
records.verdict: hit — top-1 is from an expected document
records.comment: Relevant. All three hits are from the right document and the Key Domain Vocabulary section does define backhaul. But top-1 opens mid-sentence ("time and handling risk...") because it carries an overlap tail, so the definition a reader wants is actually in top-3. Right section, imprecise chunk.

records.query: How do I stop my trucks from driving back empty after a delivery?
records.verdict: hit — top-1 is from an expected document
records.comment: Partially relevant. The paraphrase deliberately avoids the word 'backhaul' and the retriever still finds the right document, but no retrieved chunk leads with the backhaul definition, and the score falls to 0.413 from 0.536 for the same concept asked directly (q01). This is the vocabulary-mismatch penalty made visible.

records.query: Why would you keep every change as a permanent record instead of just updating a row in a table?
records.verdict: hit — top-1 is from an expected document
records.comment: Partially relevant. Top-1 is the correct document and genuinely about immutable events, but top-2 and top-3 leak into the migration and scaling documents — the paraphrase 'permanent record instead of updating a row' matches generic write-path language across the whole corpus. The only query whose top-3 spans three documents.

records.query: What has to be true for the platform to handle 5,000 requests per second?
records.verdict: hit — top-1 is from an expected document
records.comment: Relevant, but partly for the wrong reason. Top-1 directly addresses what the 5,000 requests per second figure does and does not tell you. The score (0.646) is inflated by lexical overlap: the exact phrase appears in the document title, which the breadcrumb prepends to every chunk of that document. Semantically right, but this query cannot distinguish semantic from keyword matching.

records.query: What is the best way to fine-tune a large language model on a custom dataset?
records.verdict: out-of-corpus — judged on score separation, not on document match
records.comment: Not relevant — correctly so, and this is the most useful query in the set. Nothing in a logistics corpus answers a question about fine-tuning language models, and the top score drops to 0.266, well clear of the 0.413 in-corpus floor. But the system still returns three confidently formatted results: a pure retrieval layer has no way to say 'I don't know'. A score floor near 0.35 would be needed before this feeds an LLM.
